narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors, estimated a 42% chance that you would not pass the bar on your first attempt—a risk level that exceeds our admission threshold. This prediction draws from details like your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, LSAT score of 34, Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings, family income in the upper-middle bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. Among these, the model weighed three key factors most heavily in reaching its decision: your Year 3 decile ranking, your undergraduate GPA, and your LSAT score. The standout influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 decile of 1, well below the typical student's 5.53 average, signaling to the model a significant drop in performance that often correlates with bar struggles for students in similar positions. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, above the average of 3.21, strongly pulled the prediction in the favorable direction, highlighting your solid college foundation as a positive standout. Your LSAT score of 34, though lower than the typical 36.70, also worked in your favor by nudging the odds toward passing, showing it wasn't a dealbreaker despite being below average. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile shows strengths in prior academics but a concerning Year 3 dip that outweighed them, especially for Black male applicants where consistent high performance across years tends to predict better outcomes. As a full-time student from an upper-middle income background, you're aligned with the majority who succeed, but that Year 3 ranking created the tipping point. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law—many with similar starts have rebounded strongly. To move forward, focus on boosting your recent academic decile through targeted improvement in your final undergrad year or post-bac courses, retake the LSAT to push closer to or above 37, and gather strong recommendation letters emphasizing your resilience. Reapplying after addressing that Year 3 weakness could flip the model's outlook, and we'd encourage exploring programs with holistic reviews that value your unique path. Keep pushing; your above-average GPA proves you have the capability to excel.
